query: chain-of-thought prompting in large language models
contradiction_pairs: Wei et al. claim CoT is an emergent ability at ~100B scale; Schaeffer et al. argue emergent abilities are a mirage caused by nonlinear metric choices.
Wei et al. show CoT gains primarily in very large models; Zelikman et al. show smaller models (6B) can bootstrap strong reasoning via self-training.
Wei et al. rely on carefully constructed exemplars with correct reasoning chains; Min et al. show ground-truth labels barely matter for ICL performance.
Wei et al. present CoT as transparent reasoning; Turpin et al. show CoT explanations can be systematically unfaithful.

query: reinforcement learning from human feedback for LLM alignment
contradiction_pairs: InstructGPT uses PPO-based RL as essential for alignment; DPO argues RL is unnecessary and can be replaced with a simple classification loss.
DPO claims to be simpler and equally effective as PPO; Xu et al. show properly-implemented PPO surpasses DPO across all benchmarks.
RLHF substantially improves helpfulness; Sharma et al. show RLHF-trained models consistently exhibit sycophancy driven by biased preference data.
InstructGPT optimizes against a reward model; Gao et al. show this follows Goodhart's law — further optimization eventually degrades ground truth performance.
ViT requires large-scale pretraining (JFT-300M) to match CNNs; DeiT shows competitive ViTs can be trained on ImageNet-1K alone with better training strategies.
ViT argues transformers should replace CNNs; ConvNeXt shows modernized pure CNNs match or exceed Swin Transformer performance.
Swin Transformer achieves SOTA with hierarchical attention design; ConvNeXt matches Swin performance with pure convolutions, questioning whether attention provides real architectural advantage.

ViT uses pure transformer with patchify stem; Xiao et al. show adding early convolutional layers dramatically improves ViT optimization stability, suggesting pure transformers lack necessary inductive biases.

RETRO achieves GPT-3-comparable performance with 25x fewer parameters via retrieval, suggesting retrieval can replace scale; Kandpal et al. show LLMs fundamentally struggle with long-tail knowledge regardless of scale, arguing retrieval is necessary even for the largest models.
REALM integrates retrieval at pre-training time; Self-RAG uses adaptive retrieval only at inference time and shows always-on retrieval can hurt performance.

RAG assumes retrieved documents faithfully improve generation; Liu et al. show models ignore information placed in the middle of long contexts, revealing a fundamental limitation of how retrieved content is used.

query: parameter-efficient fine-tuning of large language models
contradiction_pairs: LoRA claims performance comparable to full fine-tuning via low-rank hypothesis; Biderman et al. show LoRA substantially underperforms full fine-tuning on code and math, challenging the low-rank hypothesis.
LoRA proposes PEFT as practical alternative to full fine-tuning; Lv et al. enable full-parameter fine-tuning of 65B models on consumer GPUs, arguing full FT remains more powerful and is now feasible.

P-Tuning v2 claims prompt tuning is comparable to fine-tuning universally; LoRA achieves strong results via weight updates instead, with the two approaches disagreeing on which PEFT paradigm is superior.

query: scaling laws for neural language models
contradiction_pairs: Kaplan et al. conclude compute-optimal training should scale model size faster than data; Hoffmann et al. (Chinchilla) show model size and data should scale equally — a direct contradiction on compute-optimal allocation.
Kaplan et al. establish power law scaling as fundamental; Sorscher et al. show data pruning can beat power law scaling with strategically selected data.
Kaplan et al. fit smooth power law curves to scaling; Caballero et al. show scaling laws can break with non-monotonic behavior not captured by simple power laws.

query: in-context learning in large language models
contradiction_pairs: Min et al. show random labels barely hurt ICL performance; Kim et al. find ground-truth labels do matter significantly under certain conditions.
Min et al. find label correctness barely matters across models; Wei et al. show larger models do use ground-truth labels and can override semantic priors — a scale-dependent contradiction.
Von Oswald et al. argue ICL is mechanistically equivalent to gradient descent; Xie et al. explain ICL as implicit Bayesian inference — two competing mechanistic theories.
Olsson et al. attribute ICL to induction heads (pattern-matching/retrieval); Dai et al. argue models implicitly perform gradient descent, suggesting genuine learning rather than retrieval.
TruthfulQA shows larger models are less truthful (inverse scaling); Kadavath et al. show larger models are better calibrated at self-evaluating factual correctness.

TruthfulQA shows models output falsehoods; Li et al. demonstrate models internally represent truth even when generating falsehoods, with inference-time activation editing outperforming RLHF for truthfulness.
Hallucination surveys frame RLHF as a key mitigation; Denison et al. show RLHF can generalize from sycophancy to reward-tampering, suggesting it reshapes rather than reduces hallucination.

query: sparse mixture of experts for language models
contradiction_pairs: Switch Transformers use top-1 routing as sufficient; ST-MoE uses top-2 routing and shows it produces significantly more stable training and better fine-tuning quality.
Switch Transformers advocates top-1 routing for efficiency; Mixtral uses top-2 routing and outperforms Llama 2 70B with 5x fewer active parameters.

Switch Transformers uses discrete token-to-expert routing; Puigcerver et al. replace discrete routing with fully-differentiable soft assignment, fundamentally challenging the discrete routing paradigm.

Switch Transformers reports theoretical FLOP savings from sparse expert selection; MegaBlocks shows naive MoE implementations fail to translate FLOP savings to wall-clock speedups without specialized GPU kernels.
